evidence: Lightbox flow is split across very large adapter surfaces: `ImageLightbox` (925 LOC) builds a deeply nested input object for `useSharedLightboxState` (`src/shared/components/MediaLightbox/ImageLightbox.tsx` lines 200-260), while `useSharedLightboxState` defines a broad multi-domain contract (`src/shared/components/MediaLightbox/hooks/useSharedLightboxState.ts` lines 122-246).

Timeline orchestration exposes a very wide returned contract and composes many concerns (drag, selection, drop, viewport, endpoint, prefetch, device) from one hook (`src/tools/travel-between-images/components/Timeline/hooks/useTimelineOrchestrator.ts` lines 65-154, 156-260), with another adapter-heavy layer in `Timeline.tsx` (`src/tools/travel-between-images/components/Timeline.tsx` lines 24-92, 248-357).

`complete_task` still centralizes request parsing, auth/rate-limit checks, storage validation, task context loading, generation creation, status transitions, orchestrator follow-up, and billing follow-up in one handler (`supabase/functions/complete_task/index.ts` lines 98-394), indicating orchestration concentration despite helper extraction.

`DataFreshnessManager` bundles polling policy, realtime status state machine, circuit breaker, error classification, and toast/reporting side-effects in one singleton class (`src/shared/realtime/DataFreshnessManager.ts` lines 24-313), making policy changes tightly coupled to UX/logging behavior.

findings.summary: Lightbox abstractions rely on oversized adapter contracts that re-centralize many concerns.
evidence: `ImageLightbox` constructs a large `SharedLightboxInput` object with core/navigation/shots/layout/actions/media sections in one mapper (`ImageLightbox.tsx` lines 200-260), while the component already owns environment state, edit orchestration, and layout behavior.

`useSharedLightboxState` exposes a very broad return model (variants/navigation/star/references/lineage/shots/source/make-main/effective-media/layout/button props) in a single hook contract (`useSharedLightboxState.ts` lines 137-246).

`MediaGalleryLightbox` repeats the same adapter construction pattern and local data-shaping logic (cache subscription + media enhancement + adapter assembly) before handing off to `MediaLightboxFromAdapter` (`MediaGalleryLightbox.tsx` lines 193-237, 281-357).
findings.suggestion: Split the lightbox into explicit capability modules (for example `variants`, `shotActions`, `navigation`, `taskDetails`) with narrow typed ports, then compose these in thin container components. Replace the monolithic adapter object with feature-specific props so each caller wires only the capabilities it uses.

findings.summary: Timeline orchestration concentrates unrelated behaviors behind one expansive hook interface.
evidence: `useTimelineOrchestrator` defines a large props surface and return surface covering refs/viewport/drag/selection/pending/drop/computed/actions/endpoint/ui/device (`useTimelineOrchestrator.ts` lines 29-154).

The hook directly coordinates many subordinate hooks and mutable refs in one function (`useTimelineOrchestrator.ts` lines 187-260), so behavior changes in one concern alter the central orchestrator contract.

`Timeline.tsx` also uses grouped mega-adapters (`core`, `interactions`, `display`, `uploads`, `shotWorkflow`, `segmentNavigation`) and performs substantial translation into a `MediaLightboxInputAdapter` (`Timeline.tsx` lines 24-92, 248-357), duplicating orchestration at component level.
findings.suggestion: Refactor timeline orchestration into composable controllers with stable boundaries (e.g., `useTimelineInteractionController`, `useTimelineViewportController`, `useTimelineLightboxController`) and expose minimal, feature-scoped return contracts. Keep `Timeline.tsx` as a view composition layer rather than another adapter assembler.

findings.summary: The complete-task edge handler still mixes too many workflow phases in one procedure.
evidence: `completeTaskHandler` performs end-to-end control flow from auth/rate limit to storage checks, task context loading, optional params mutation, generation creation, task completion DB write, orchestrator completion, billing follow-up, and response assembly in one handler (`index.ts` lines 98-394).

Even with extracted helpers, the top-level function remains a sequential 15-step transactional script (`index.ts` comments/steps around lines 122-375), making it hard to reason about failure boundaries and ownership.

`orchestrator.ts` itself contains additional policy branching and persistence behavior (`orchestrator.ts` lines 131-315), reinforcing a layered orchestration stack where policy and IO remain tightly coupled.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a pipeline-style application service with explicit stage boundaries (`validateRequest`, `authorizeActor`, `resolveStorage`, `persistTaskCompletion`, `postCompletionHooks`) and stage result types. Keep edge handlers thin and move policy/side-effect choreography into reusable domain workflows with focused test seams.

findings.summary: DataFreshnessManager conflates policy engine, state store, and user-facing error behavior.
evidence: `DataFreshnessManager` stores mutable realtime/query state and also computes polling intervals, classifies errors, enforces circuit breaker policy, and triggers toast/log reporting (`DataFreshnessManager.ts` lines 24-313).

Methods like `onFetchFailure` both mutate circuit-breaker counters and dispatch user-facing error reporting (`DataFreshnessManager.ts` lines 162-213), coupling reliability policy to presentation side-effects.

A similar pattern of module-level mutable caches plus cross-cutting behavior appears in `toolSettingsService` (`inflightSettingsFetches`, `cachedUser`, unknown-id reporting; `toolSettingsService.ts` lines 31-61, 71-80), suggesting recurring stateful service design without clear boundary separation.
findings.suggestion: Split freshness concerns into separate units: a pure polling-policy evaluator, a state repository/store, and a notifier/logger adapter. Keep singleton orchestration thin and injectable so policy changes and UX/reporting changes can evolve independently.
